note: A country dance song of obscure meaning — 'popping the weasel' may reference pawning a tailor's iron. Public domain across all its traditional verses.
verses:
Half a pound of tuppenny rice,
Half a pound of treacle,
That's the way the money goes,
Pop! goes the weasel.

Up and down the City Road,
In and out the Eagle,
That's the way the money goes,
Pop! goes the weasel.

A penny for a spool of thread,
A penny for a needle,
That's the way the money goes,
Pop! goes the weasel.

title: The Girl I Left Behind Me
origin: Traditional Irish/British march tune, in use by British and American armies since at least the late 18th century; earliest printed lyrics c. 1791
note: A marching tune played by regiments departing for war on both sides of the Atlantic, from the American Revolution through the Civil War and beyond. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
I'm lonesome since I crossed the hill,
And o'er the moor and valley,
Such heavy thoughts my heart do fill,
Since parting with my Sally.
I seek no more the fine or gay,
For each does but remind me
How swift the hours did pass away
With the girl I left behind me.

Oh, ne'er shall I forget the night
The stars were bright above me,
And gently lent their silvery light
When first she vowed she loved me.
But now I'm bound to Brighton camp,
Good Lord, may weather find me,
And send me safely back again
To the girl I left behind me.

title: Turkey in the Straw
origin: Traditional American fiddle tune, melody traceable to the 1820s-30s Irish/British jig tradition; the innocuous "turkey" verses were widely circulated in print by the early 1900s
note: A staple of American fiddle music. The tune was also used for the separately-titled, racially offensive minstrel song "Zip Coon" (c. 1834) — the verses presented here are the unrelated, family-friendly "turkey" lyrics in standard circulation today, not that minstrel text.
verses:
Turkey in the straw, turkey in the hay,
Roll 'em up and twist 'em up a high tuckahaw,
And hit 'em up a tune called Turkey in the Straw.

Went out to milk, and I didn't know how,
I milked the goat instead of the cow,
A monkey sittin' on a pile of straw,
A-winkin' at his mother-in-law.

title: Skip to My Lou
origin: Traditional American play-party song, documented in the Midwest by the 1840s; first collected in print by the early 20th century
note: A 'play-party' song — sung a cappella for dancing games in communities where instrumental dance music was frowned upon. Public domain as an anonymous traditional song.
verses:
Lost my partner, what'll I do?
Lost my partner, what'll I do?
Lost my partner, what'll I do?
Skip to my Lou, my darling.

Skip, skip, skip to my Lou,
Skip, skip, skip to my Lou,
Skip, skip, skip to my Lou,
Skip to my Lou, my darling.

I'll get another one prettier than you,
I'll get another one prettier than you,
I'll get another one prettier than you,
Skip to my Lou, my darling.

title: She'll Be Comin' Round the Mountain
origin: Derived from the African American spiritual "When the Chariot Comes," published 1899 in William E. Barton's Old Plantation Hymns; secular verses adapted by Midwestern railroad workers in the 1890s, first printed in Carl Sandburg's The American Songbag, 1927
note: Traces to a spiritual about the Second Coming, reworked into a secular folk favorite. Both the underlying 1899 spiritual and the 1927 printed secular version are public domain.
verses:
She'll be comin' round the mountain when she comes,
She'll be comin' round the mountain when she comes,
She'll be comin' round the mountain,
She'll be comin' round the mountain,
She'll be comin' round the mountain when she comes.

She'll be drivin' six white horses when she comes,
She'll be drivin' six white horses when she comes,
She'll be drivin' six white horses,
She'll be drivin' six white horses,
She'll be drivin' six white horses when she comes.

We will all go out to meet her when she comes,
We will all go out to meet her when she comes,
We will all go out to meet her,
We will all go out to meet her,
We will all go out to meet her when she comes.

title: Buffalo Gals
origin: Written by John Hodges (performing as "Cool White") as "Lubly Fan," copyrighted 1844
note: Originally a minstrel-stage song later adapted into countless local variants ("Buffalo gals," "Alabama gals," etc.) as it traveled. The verses below are the commonly sung, non-dialect chorus and verse in standard circulation.
verses:
As I was walking down the street,
Down the street, down the street,
A pretty little girl I chanced to meet,
Under the silvery moon.

Buffalo gals, won't you come out tonight,
Come out tonight, come out tonight,
Buffalo gals, won't you come out tonight,
And dance by the light of the moon?

I asked her if she'd have a talk,
Have a talk, have a talk,
Her feet took up the whole sidewalk,
And left no room for me.

title: Old Dan Tucker
origin: Attributed to Dan Emmett, copyrighted 1843
note: One of the most popular songs of the 1840s minstrel stage, later absorbed into square dance and children's song repertoires. Presented here with the commonly sung, non-dialect verses in wide circulation today.
verses:
Old Dan Tucker was a fine old man,
Washed his face in a frying pan,
Combed his hair with a wagon wheel,
And died with a toothache in his heel.

Get out the way, old Dan Tucker,
You're too late to get your supper,
Supper's over and breakfast's cookin',
Old Dan Tucker just stands there lookin'.

Old Dan Tucker came to town,
Riding a billy goat, leading a hound,
The hound dog barked, the billy goat jumped,
And threw old Dan across a stump.

title: Jeanie with the Light Brown Hair
origin: Stephen Foster, published 1854 by Firth, Pond & Co., New York
note: Written for Foster's estranged wife Jane; became the official state song of Kentucky (as 'My Old Kentucky Home' companion) in the 20th century. Public domain since its 1854 publication.
verses:
I dream of Jeanie with the light brown hair,
Borne, like a vapor, on the summer air;
I see her tripping where the bright streams play,
Happy as the daisies that dance on her way.

Many were the wild notes her merry voice would pour,
Many were the blithe birds that warbled them o'er:
Oh! I dream of Jeanie with the light brown hair,
Floating, like a vapor, on the soft summer air.

I long for Jeanie with the daydawn smile,
Radiant in gladness, warm with winning guile;
I hear her melodies, like joys gone by,
Sighing round my heart o'er the fond hopes that die.

title: My Darling Nelly Gray
origin: Benjamin Russell Hanby, published 1856 by Oliver Ditson & Co., Boston
note: Written by an Ohio abolitionist minister's son about a slave family torn apart by sale, decades before the Civil War. Public domain since its 1856 publication.
verses:
There's a low green valley on the old Kentucky shore,
There I've whiled many happy hours away,
A-sitting and a-singing by the little cottage door,
Where lived my darling Nelly Gray.

Oh, my poor Nelly Gray, they have taken you away,
And I'll never see my darling any more;
I'm sitting by the river and I'm weeping all the day,
For you've gone from the old Kentucky shore.

One night I went to see her, but she's gone, the neighbors say,
The white man bound her with his chain;
They have taken her to Georgia for to wear her life away,
As she toils in the cotton and the cane.

title: When Johnny Comes Marching Home
origin: Patrick Sarsfield Gilmore, published 1863 (under the pen name Louis Lambert)
note: Written by a Union bandmaster, possibly adapted from the older Irish tune 'Johnny I Hardly Knew Ye.' Public domain since its 1863 publication.
verses:
When Johnny comes marching home again,
Hurrah! Hurrah!
We'll give him a hearty welcome then,
Hurrah! Hurrah!
The men will cheer, the boys will shout,
The ladies they will all turn out,
And we'll all feel gay
When Johnny comes marching home.

The old church bell will peal with joy,
Hurrah! Hurrah!
To welcome home our darling boy,
Hurrah! Hurrah!
The village lads and lassies say,
With roses they will strew the way,
And we'll all feel gay
When Johnny comes marching home.

Get ready for the jubilee,
Hurrah! Hurrah!
We'll give the hero three times three,
Hurrah! Hurrah!
The laurel wreath is ready now
To place upon his loyal brow,
And we'll all feel gay
When Johnny comes marching home.

title: Tenting on the Old Camp Ground
origin: Walter Kittredge, published 1864
note: Written by a Union soldier and popular on both sides of the Civil War as a weary plea for peace. Public domain since its 1860s publication.
verses:
We're tenting tonight on the old camp ground,
Give us a song to cheer
Our weary hearts, a song of home,
And friends we love so dear.

Many are the hearts that are weary tonight,
Wishing for the war to cease;
Many are the hearts looking for the right,
To see the dawn of peace.
Tenting tonight, tenting tonight,
Tenting on the old camp ground.

We've been tenting tonight on the old camp ground,
Thinking of days gone by,
Of the loved ones at home that gave us the hand,
And the tear that said goodbye.

title: The Battle Cry of Freedom
origin: George Frederick Root, published 1862 by Root & Cady, Chicago
note: One of the most popular songs of the Union Army, with a separate Confederate-lyric variant sung to the same tune. The 1862 original is public domain.
verses:
Yes, we'll rally round the flag, boys, we'll rally once again,
Shouting the battle cry of freedom,
We will rally from the hillside, we'll gather from the plain,
Shouting the battle cry of freedom.

The Union forever! Hurrah, boys, hurrah!
Down with the traitor, up with the star;
While we rally round the flag, boys, we rally once again,
Shouting the battle cry of freedom.

We are springing to the call of our brothers gone before,
Shouting the battle cry of freedom,
And we'll fill the vacant ranks with a million freemen more,
Shouting the battle cry of freedom.

title: Daisy Bell (A Bicycle Built for Two)
origin: Harry Dacre, published 1892
note: Written during the 1890s bicycle craze; later famous as the song IBM's computer HAL 9000 sings while being shut down in 2001: A Space Odyssey (1968). Public domain since 1892.
verses:
Daisy, Daisy, give me your answer, do!
I'm half crazy, all for the love of you!
It won't be a stylish marriage,
I can't afford a carriage,
But you'll look sweet upon the seat
Of a bicycle built for two.

We will go 'tandem' as man and wife,
Daisy, Daisy!
Peddling away down the road of life,
I and my Daisy Bell!
When the road's dark, we can both despise
P'licemen and lamps as well;
There are 'bright lights' in the dazzling eyes
Of beautiful Daisy Bell!

title: After the Ball
origin: Charles K. Harris, published 1891
note: One of the biggest hits of the 1890s, selling over five million copies of sheet music and helping establish Tin Pan Alley. Public domain since its 1891 publication.
verses:
A little maiden climbed an old man's knee,
Begged for a story, "Do, Uncle, please.
Why are you single; why live alone?
Have you no babies; have you no home?"

After the ball is over, after the break of morn,
After the dancers' leaving, after the stars are gone,
Many a heart is aching, if you could read them all,
Many the hopes that have vanished, after the ball.

"I have a story, sweetheart," he said,
"A story that's sad, and old as the days;
It was many years ago I loved a maid,
Beautiful, one who is now passed away."

title: The Sidewalks of New York
origin: Words by James W. Blake, music by Charles B. Lawlor, published 1894
note: A nostalgic waltz that became an unofficial anthem of New York City, later used as Al Smith's 1928 presidential campaign song. Public domain since 1894.
verses:
Down in front of Casey's old brown wooden stoop,
On a summer's evening we formed a merry group,
Boys and girls together, we would sing and waltz,
While Tony played the organ on the sidewalks of New York.

East side, west side, all around the town,
The tots sang 'ring-a-rosie', 'London Bridge is falling down,'
Boys and girls together, me and Mamie O'Rourke,
Tripped the light fantastic on the sidewalks of New York.

Things have changed since those times, some are up in 'G',
Others they are wand'rers, but they all feel just like me,
They'd part with all they've got, could they once more walk,
With their best girl and have a twirl on the sidewalks of New York.

title: In the Good Old Summer Time
origin: Words by Ren Shields, music by George Evans, published 1902
note: A hit of the early Tin Pan Alley era, still played at ballparks and county fairs. Public domain since 1902.
verses:
There's a time in each year that we always hold dear,
Good old summer time,
With the birds and the trees'es and sweet scented breezes,
Good old summer time.

In the good old summer time,
In the good old summer time,
Strolling through the shady lanes with your baby mine,
You hold her hand and she holds yours,
And that's a very good sign
That she's your tootsie wootsie in the good old summer time.

When your day's work is over then you are in clover,
And life is one beautiful rhyme,
No trouble annoying, each one is enjoying
The good old summer time.

title: Bill Bailey, Won't You Please Come Home
origin: Hughie Cannon, published 1902
note: One of the most recorded songs of the Tin Pan Alley era, later a jazz standard covered by Louis Armstrong, Bobby Darin, and countless others. Public domain since 1902.
verses:
On one summer's day, sun was shining fine,
The lady love of old Bill Bailey was hanging out clothes on the line,
In her back yard, and weeping hard,
She married a butcher, but he's her man,
And to a friend she said, in tears,"I'm all alone,"
I've been savin' this ad through the years.

Won't you come home, Bill Bailey, won't you come home?
She moans the whole day long,
I'll do the cooking, honey, I'll pay the rent,
I know I've done you wrong,
'Member that rainy evenin' I threw you out,
With nothin' but a fine tooth comb,
I know I'm to blame, well, ain't that a shame,
Bill Bailey, won't you please come home.

title: Meet Me in St. Louis, Louis
origin: Words by Andrew B. Sterling, music by Kerry Mills, published 1904
note: Written for the 1904 St. Louis World's Fair, and still associated with the city today. Public domain since 1904.
verses:
When Louis came home to the flat,
He hung up his coat and his hat,
He gazed all around, but no wifey he found,
So he said, "Where can Flossie be at?"
A note on the table he spied,
He read it just once, then he cried:
It read, "Louis dear, it's too slow for me here,
So I think I will go on my way."

Meet me in St. Louis, Louis,
Meet me at the fair,
Don't tell me the lights are shining
Any place but there;
We will dance the hoochee koochee,
I will be your tootsie wootsie,
If you will meet me in St. Louis, Louis,
Meet me at the fair.

"Meet me down at the Union Station,
Honey, dear, don't be late;
Meet me down at the Union Station,
By the big clock wait;
Meet me at the fountain there, dear,
Or I will meet you at the gate,
Meet me in St. Louis, Louis,
Meet me at the fair."

title: Red Wing
origin: Words by Thurland Chattaway, music by Kerry Mills, published 1907
note: One of the best-known 'Indian intermezzo' songs of the Tin Pan Alley era; its melody was later reused for the labor anthem 'Union Maid.' Public domain since 1907.
verses:
Now the moon shines tonight on pretty Red Wing,
The breeze is sighing, the night bird's crying,
Far away her warrior's sleeping,
While Red Wing's weeping her heart away.

She loved a warrior bold,
This shy little maid of old,
But brave and gay he rode one day,
To the battle far away.

Now the moon shines tonight on pretty Red Wing,
The breeze is sighing, the night bird's crying,
Far away her warrior's sleeping,
While Red Wing's weeping her heart away.

title: Take Me Out to the Ball Game
origin: Words by Jack Norworth, music by Albert Von Tilzer, published 1908
note: Sung during the seventh-inning stretch at ballparks across America; neither writer had attended a professional game before writing it. Public domain since 1908.
verses:
Katie Casey was baseball mad,
Had the fever and had it bad,
Just to root for the home town crew,
Ev'ry sou Katie blew.
On a Saturday, her young beau
Called to see if she'd like to go
To see a show, but Miss Kate said, "No,
I'll tell you what you can do."

Take me out to the ball game,
Take me out with the crowd,
Buy me some peanuts and cracker jack,
I don't care if I never get back,
Let me root, root, root for the home team,
If they don't win, it's a shame,
For it's one, two, three strikes, you're out,
At the old ball game.

title: Shine On, Harvest Moon
origin: Words by Jack Norworth, music by Nora Bayes, published 1908
note: Introduced by husband-and-wife vaudeville stars Norworth and Bayes; later a standard revived in film and jazz. Public domain since 1908.
verses:
I ain't had no lovin' since April, January, June, or July,
Snow time ain't no time to stay outdoors and spoon,
So shine on, shine on harvest moon, for me and my gal.

Shine on, shine on harvest moon,
Up in the sky,
I ain't had no lovin' since January, February, June or July,
Snow time ain't no time to stay outdoors and spoon,
So shine on, shine on harvest moon,
For me and my gal.

title: Home! Sweet Home!
origin: Words by John Howard Payne, music adapted by Henry Bishop, from the opera Clari, or the Maid of Milan, 1823
note: One of the most popular songs of the 19th century, played to remind soldiers of home on both sides of the Civil War (and reportedly banned from Union camps to prevent desertion). Public domain since 1823.
verses:
'Mid pleasures and palaces though we may roam,
Be it ever so humble, there's no place like home;
A charm from the skies seems to hallow us there,
Which, seek through the world, is ne'er met with elsewhere.

Home, home, sweet, sweet home!
There's no place like home!
There's no place like home!

title: The Last Rose of Summer
origin: Thomas Moore, published 1805 in his Irish Melodies, set to a traditional Irish air
note: One of Thomas Moore's most famous Irish Melodies, later quoted by Beethoven and Flotow. Public domain since its 1805 publication.
verses:
'Tis the last rose of summer,
Left blooming alone;
All her lovely companions
Are faded and gone;
No flower of her kindred,
No rosebud is nigh,
To reflect back her blushes,
Or give sigh for sigh.

I'll not leave thee, thou lone one,
To pine on the stem;
Since the lovely are sleeping,
Go, sleep thou with them;
Thus kindly I scatter
Thy leaves o'er the bed,
Where thy mates of the garden
Lie scentless and dead.

title: The Minstrel Boy
origin: Thomas Moore, published 1813 in his Irish Melodies, set to the traditional Irish air "The Moreen"
note: Written in memory of friends Moore lost in the 1798 Irish Rebellion; long a favorite at military funerals. Public domain since its 1813 publication.
verses:
The Minstrel Boy to the war is gone,
In the ranks of death you'll find him;
His father's sword he has girded on,
And his wild harp slung behind him.
"Land of song!" said the warrior bard,
"Though all the world betrays thee,
One sword, at least, thy rights shall guard,
One faithful harp shall praise thee!"

The Minstrel fell! But the foeman's chain
Could not bring his proud soul under;
The harp he loved ne'er spoke again,
For he tore its chords asunder;
And said, "No chains shall sully thee,
Thou soul of love and bravery!
Thy songs were made for the pure and free,
They shall never sound in slavery."

title: The Wild Rover
origin: Traditional British Isles song; earliest known printed text 1829 (as a temperance ballad), Roud Folk Song Index no. 1173
note: Often linked to the 19th-century temperance movement despite its rowdy reputation today; the traditional song has no identified author and is public domain.
verses:
I've been a wild rover for many a year,
And I've spent all my money on whiskey and beer,
But now I'm returning with gold in great store,
And I never will play the wild rover no more.

And it's no, nay, never,
No, nay, never, no more,
Will I play the wild rover,
No, never, no more.

I went into an alehouse I used to frequent,
And I told the landlady my money was spent,
I asked her for credit, she answered me "Nay,
Such a custom as yours I could have any day."

title: Paddy Works on the Railway
origin: Traditional Irish-American railroad work song; documented in print by 1864 as "Paddy on the Railway"
note: Sung by the Irish immigrant laborers who built much of the American and Canadian rail network in the mid-19th century. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
In eighteen hundred and forty-one,
I put my corduroy breeches on,
I put my corduroy breeches on,
To work upon the railway, the railway,
I'm weary of the railway,
Poor Paddy works on the railway.

In eighteen hundred and forty-two,
From Hartlepool I moved to Crewe,
And I found myself a job to do,
A-working on the railway.

In eighteen hundred and forty-three,
I broke my shin at the knee,
I broke my shin at the knee,
A-working on the railway.
